issues.current_text: the start index of the matched substring for group `i`, or -1 if the group did not match
issues.problem: The class-level description for `Match.start(i)` (line 794) does not mention the -1 case, unlike its `MatchData` counterpart. The @return introduces this detail not in the prose. While accurate (the underlying Java `Matcher.start(i)` returns -1 for unmatched groups), it creates a slight asymmetry between the description and @return.
issues.suggestion: Consider keeping as is or aligning with MatchData phrasing: 'the index of the first matched character for group `i`, or -1 if the group did not match'.

issues.current_text: the index following the last matched character for group `i`, or -1 if the group did not match
issues.problem: Same as above: the class-level description (line 801) does not mention the -1 case, so the @return adds information beyond the prose. Acceptable but slightly inconsistent with how the class describes the method.
issues.suggestion: Keep as is; the added detail is useful and matches the underlying Java behavior.

issues.current_text: the names of the capturing groups, used to look up groups by name
issues.problem: The corresponding `_groupNames` parameter on `MatchIterator` (line 862) is documented as 'the names of the capturing groups, if any'. The wording differs between the two related classes for what is essentially the same parameter.
issues.suggestion: For consistency, consider: 'the names of the capturing groups, if any, used to look up groups by name'.

issues.current_text: the start index of the matched substring for group `i`, or -1 if the group did not match
issues.problem: Minor terminology inconsistency: the description uses 'index of the first matched character' while the @return uses 'start index of the matched substring'. Both are accurate but phrased differently.
issues.suggestion: Consider: 'the index of the first matched character in group `i`, or -1 if the group did not match' to match the prose.

general_feedback: The filled-in @return and @param documentation is accurate, clear, and uses consistent backtick formatting for code references and parameter names. All entries correctly describe the -1/null sentinel behavior for unmatched groups. The MatchIterator variants helpfully add 'in the current match' to distinguish iterator semantics. Only minor stylistic suggestions: a few terminology choices ('start index of the matched substring' vs. 'index of the first matched character') diverge slightly from existing prose, and the `_groupNames` description could be aligned across `Match` and `MatchIterator`. No factual errors.
